changed_functions.name: CdAddAllocationFromDirent
key_changes: ROOT CAUSE FIX: Allocation size computation changed from 32-bit (lea eax,[rax+rax*4]; shl eax,0x4) to 64-bit (lea r10,[rax+rax*4]; shl r10,0x4), preventing integer overflow in the ExAllocatePoolWithTag size parameter.

ADDED OVERFLOW CHECK: New cmp r10,rdx / cmovbe eax,r10d / ja CdRaiseStatusEx sequence at 0x1c000a4b0-0x1c000a4b7. If the 64-bit product (count * 0x50) exceeds 0xFFFFFFFF, raises STATUS_UNEXPECTED_IO_ERROR (0xC00000E9) via CdRaiseStatusEx and does NOT return.
Stack frame increased from 0x20 to 0x30 bytes to accommodate the new overflow check logic and error path.
Error path at 0x1c000a5fa calls CdRaiseStatusEx(arg1, 0xC00000E9, 0, 0x20000, 0x194) — this is the new guard that was entirely absent in the unpatched binary.
changed_functions.security_notes: Integer overflow in pool allocation size. The unpatched code computes the new allocation array size as (capacity * 5) << 4 in 32-bit eax, which silently wraps when capacity >= ~53.7 million. This truncated size is passed to ExAllocatePoolWithTag, allocating a far-too-small kernel pool buffer. The subsequent memmove (at 0x1c000a4e6) computes the copy length as (capacity * 5) << 3 in 64-bit r8, which does NOT overflow, writing capacity * 0x28 bytes (potentially gigabytes) into the undersized buffer. This is a kernel pool buffer overflow (heap overflow) in the CDFS filesystem driver, triggered by parsing a crafted ISO9660 disk image with a file containing an enormous number of directory extents.
changed_functions.assembly_diff:
UNPATCHED (0x1c000a46c) — vulnerable 32-bit allocation size computation:
0x1c000a485  mov     eax, dword [rdx+0x108]      ; eax = capacity (32-bit count)
0x1c000a49d  lea     eax, [rax+rax*4]            ; eax = count * 5 (32-BIT, OVERFLOWS)
0x1c000a4a6  shl     eax, 0x4                    ; eax = count * 0x50 (32-BIT, OVERFLOWS)
0x1c000a4ae  mov     edx, eax                    ; edx = truncated size for ExAllocatePoolWithTag
0x1c000a4b2  call    qword [rel 0x1c0008040]     ; ExAllocatePoolWithTag(0x11, truncated_size, 'CdmA')
...
0x1c000a4ce  mov     ecx, dword [rdi+0x108]      ; ecx = count (32-bit, zero-extended to rcx)
0x1c000a4db  lea     r8, [rcx+rcx*4]             ; r8 = count * 5 (64-BIT, NO OVERFLOW)
0x1c000a4e2  shl     r8, 0x3                     ; r8 = count * 0x28 (64-BIT, FULL SIZE)
0x1c000a4e6  call    0x1c0002e40                 ; memmove(new_buf, old_buf, count*0x28) → OVERFLOW

PATCHED (0x1c000a470) — safe 64-bit computation + overflow check:
0x1c000a489  mov     eax, dword [rdx+0x108]      ; eax = capacity
0x1c000a4a1  lea     r10, [rax+rax*4]            ; r10 = count * 5 (64-BIT)
0x1c000a4a5  mov     edx, 0xffffffff             ; edx = 0xFFFFFFFF (max 32-bit)
0x1c000a4aa  shl     r10, 0x4                    ; r10 = count * 0x50 (64-BIT, NO OVERFLOW)
0x1c000a4ae  mov     eax, edx                    ; eax = 0xFFFFFFFF (default)
0x1c000a4b0  cmp     r10, rdx                    ; compare 64-bit size vs 0xFFFFFFFF
0x1c000a4b3  cmovbe  eax, r10d                   ; if size <= 0xFFFFFFFF, use actual size
0x1c000a4b7  ja      0x1c000a5fa                 ; if size > 0xFFFFFFFF → ERROR PATH
...
0x1c000a5fa  mov     r9d, 0x20000                ; CdRaiseStatusEx args
0x1c000a600  mov     dword [rsp+0x20], 0x194     ; line number 404
0x1c000a60b  mov     edx, 0xc00000e9             ; STATUS_UNEXPECTED_IO_ERROR
0x1c000a610  call    0x1c00010e0                 ; CdRaiseStatusEx(arg1, ...) — ABORTS

changed_functions.name: CdInitializeFcbFromFileContext
key_changes: Register allocation swap (r12 ↔ r13) — cosmetic, due to changed stack frame in CdAddAllocationFromDirent.

Call to CdAddAllocationFromDirent updated to pass arg1 (IRP context) as first argument instead of a stale return value from CdUpdateDirentFromRawDirent — needed because the patched CdAddAllocationFromDirent now calls CdRaiseStatusEx(arg1,...) which requires the proper IRP request context.
Call target updated from 0x1c000a46c to 0x1c000a470 (patched function address).
CdUpdateDirentFromRawDirent return value no longer assigned to a variable that was passed as arg1 — correctness fix supporting the new error handling path.

changed_functions.name: CdLookupAllocation
key_changes: Register allocation swap (r14 ↔ rsi) — cosmetic, due to compiler reassignment.

Call to CdAddAllocationFromDirent updated to pass arg1 (original IRP context) as first argument instead of stale return value rcx_21 from CdUpdateDirentFromRawDirent — same correctness fix as CdInitializeFcbFromFileContext.
CdRaiseStatusEx line number constants incremented by 1 (0xd7→0xd8, 0x12c→0x12d) due to source line shifts.
ExAcquireFastMutex return value no longer assigned to a variable used as arg1.

security_findings.entry_point: IRP_MJ_CREATE / IRP_MJ_READ on a mounted ISO9660 volume (crafted disk image)
call_chain: IRP_MJ_CREATE or IRP_MJ_READ dispatched to CdFsdDispatch (0x1c0001130) / CdFspDispatch (0x1c0002200)
security_findings.vulnerability_class: integer overflow leading to kernel pool buffer overflow (CWE-190 / CWE-122)
security_findings.description: The function CdAddAllocationFromDirent grows an allocation extent array for a file on an ISO9660 volume. The array capacity at FCB+0x108 is initialized to 1 and doubles on each growth (mov esi,1 at the FCB-init site; add eax,eax in the growth path), so it only ever holds power-of-two values. When the current index reaches the capacity, it allocates a new doubled array. The allocation size is computed as (capacity * 5) << 4 = capacity * 0x50 using 32-bit arithmetic (lea eax,[rax+rax*4]; shl eax,0x4 at 0x1c000a49d-0x1c000a4a6). This 32-bit product overflows once capacity exceeds 0xFFFFFFFF/0x50 (~53.7 million); because capacity is a power of two, the first value that reaches this is 0x4000000 (2^26). At capacity 0x4000000, capacity * 0x50 = 0x140000000 truncates to 0x40000000 (1 GiB), which is passed to ExAllocatePoolWithTag. However, the subsequent memmove that copies the old array data into the new buffer computes its length as (capacity * 5) << 3 = capacity * 0x28 using 64-bit arithmetic (lea r8,[rcx+rcx*4]; shl r8,0x3 at 0x1c000a4db-0x1c000a4e2), which does NOT overflow. This causes memmove to write capacity * 0x28 = 0xA0000000 (~2.5 GiB) into the 1 GiB buffer, overrunning it by ~1.5 GiB — a kernel pool buffer overflow. The overrun source is the old extent array, whose entries hold directory extent data (file offsets, sizes, interleave parameters parsed from the ISO9660 on-disk directory records), so the copied content is influenced by the crafted image, but the overrun is a single unbounded linear copy, not a bounded or targetable write.
security_findings.trigger_conditions:
1) Craft an ISO9660 disk image containing a file with an extraordinarily large number of directory entry extents (allocation runs). This can be achieved via many small interleaved extents or a massive multi-extent file. The capacity counter at FCB+0x108 starts at 1 and doubles (1→2→4→...→2^n) as dirents are processed.
2) The capacity must reach 0x4000000 (2^26 = 67,108,864) for the overflow: at that value capacity * 0x50 = 0x140000000 truncates to 0x40000000 (1 GiB) in 32-bit, while the 64-bit memmove copies capacity * 0x28 = 0xA0000000 (~2.5 GiB), overrunning the buffer by ~1.5 GiB. This is the first (and only readily reachable) overflow point, because capacity only takes power-of-two values and the previous growth at 0x2000000 (which needs a ~2.5 GiB paged-pool allocation to succeed) must complete first.
3) Reaching this growth requires index to reach 2^26, i.e. on the order of 67 million dirents parsed from the on-disk directory records.
4) Mount the crafted ISO image (via Windows built-in ISO mounting, third-party virtual drive, or physical optical media) and open/read a file on it to trigger IRP_MJ_CREATE or IRP_MJ_READ dispatch into CDFS.
5) The dirents are parsed from the on-disk ISO9660 directory records; the attacker controls their content (extent LBN, data length, interleave gap/unit, continuation flags).
security_findings.primitive: Kernel pool buffer overflow. The demonstrable, reachable impact is kernel memory corruption leading to a system crash (BSOD / denial of service), reachable by any user who can mount and access a crafted ISO. The overflow is a single ~1.5 GiB linear memmove that runs off the end of the pool region into unmapped memory; its length cannot be reduced (the smallest reachable overrun is already ~1.5 GiB) and it cannot be groomed, so it is not a controllable write primitive. Reliable privilege escalation is not demonstrated. The additional preconditions (parsing ~67 million dirents and a prior successful ~2.5 GiB paged-pool allocation) further constrain the vulnerability to a DoS in practice.
security_findings.assembly:
UNPATCHED CdAddAllocationFromDirent full assembly at 0x1c000a46c (cdfs_unpatched.sys):

0x1c000a46c  mov     rax, rsp
0x1c000a46f  mov     qword [rax+0x8], rbx
0x1c000a473  mov     qword [rax+0x10], rbp
0x1c000a477  mov     qword [rax+0x18], rsi
0x1c000a47b  mov     qword [rax+0x20], rdi
0x1c000a47f  push    r14
0x1c000a481  sub     rsp, 0x20
0x1c000a485  mov     eax, dword [rdx+0x108]        ; eax = capacity (arg2+0x108)
0x1c000a48b  mov     r14, r9                       ; r14 = arg4 (file offset)
0x1c000a48e  mov     ebp, r8d                      ; ebp = arg3 (dirent index)
0x1c000a491  mov     rdi, rdx                      ; rdi = arg2 (FCB pointer)
0x1c000a494  cmp     r8d, eax                      ; index >= capacity?
0x1c000a497  jb      0x1c000a52f                   ; if index < capacity, skip growth
; --- GROWTH PATH: allocate doubled array ---
0x1c000a49d  lea     eax, [rax+rax*4]              ; *** 32-BIT: eax = capacity * 5 (product overflows once capacity > ~53.7M; first reachable at power-of-two capacity 0x4000000)
0x1c000a4a0  mov     r8d, 0x616d6443               ; pool tag 'CdmA'
0x1c000a4a6  shl     eax, 0x4                      ; *** 32-BIT: eax = capacity * 0x50 (OVERFLOWS — truncated allocation size)
0x1c000a4a9  mov     ecx, 0x11                     ; PoolType = PagedPool
0x1c000a4ae  mov     edx, eax                      ; edx = truncated size
0x1c000a4b0  mov     ebx, eax                      ; ebx = truncated size (for memset)
0x1c000a4b2  call    qword [rel 0x1c0008040]       ; ExAllocatePoolWithTag(PagedPool, truncated_size, 'CdmA')
0x1c000a4b9  nop
0x1c000a4be  mov     r8d, ebx                      ; r8d = truncated size
0x1c000a4c1  xor     edx, edx
0x1c000a4c3  mov     rcx, rax                      ; rcx = new buffer
0x1c000a4c6  mov     rsi, rax                      ; rsi = new buffer
0x1c000a4c9  call    0x1c0003180                   ; memset(new_buf, 0, truncated_size)
; --- memmove old array into new (UNdersized) buffer ---
0x1c000a4ce  mov     ecx, dword [rdi+0x108]        ; ecx = capacity (32-bit, zero-extended to rcx)
0x1c000a4d4  mov     rdx, qword [rdi+0x110]        ; rdx = old array pointer
0x1c000a4db  lea     r8, [rcx+rcx*4]               ; *** 64-BIT: r8 = capacity * 5 (NO OVERFLOW)
0x1c000a4df  mov     rcx, rsi                      ; rcx = destination (undersized new buffer)
0x1c000a4e2  shl     r8, 0x3                       ; *** 64-BIT: r8 = capacity * 0x28 (FULL SIZE — causes POOL OVERFLOW)
0x1c000a4e6  call    0x1c0002e40                   ; memmove(new_buf, old_array, capacity*0x28) → HEAP OVERFLOW
0x1c000a4eb  mov     eax, dword [rdi+0x108]        ; eax = capacity
0x1c000a4f1  cmp     eax, 0x1
0x1c000a4f4  je      0x1c000a51e                   ; if capacity == 1, skip free
0x1c000a4f6  mov     rcx, qword [rdi+0x110]        ; rcx = old array
0x1c000a4fd  test    rcx, rcx
0x1c000a500  je      0x1c000a51e
0x1c000a502  xor     edx, edx
0x1c000a504  call    qword [rel 0x1c0008038]       ; ExFreePoolWithTag(old_array, 0)
0x1c000a50b  nop
0x1c000a510  and     qword [rdi+0x110], 0x0        ; clear old array pointer
0x1c000a518  mov     eax, dword [rdi+0x108]        ; eax = capacity
0x1c000a51e  add     eax, eax                      ; new capacity = old * 2
0x1c000a520  mov     qword [rdi+0x110], rsi        ; store new array pointer
0x1c000a527  mov     dword [rdi+0x108], eax        ; store new capacity
0x1c000a52d  jmp     0x1c000a536
; --- Write dirent data into array at index ---
0x1c000a52f  mov     rsi, qword [rdx+0x110]        ; rsi = existing array
0x1c000a536  mov     r10, qword [rsp+0x50]         ; r10 = arg5 (dirent data pointer)
0x1c000a53b  lea     r9, [rbp*4]                   ; r9 = index * 4
0x1c000a543  mov     rax, qword [rdi+0x68]         ; rax = Vcb pointer
0x1c000a547  add     r9, rbp                       ; r9 = index * 5
0x1c000a54a  mov     edx, dword [r10+0x8]          ; edx = dirent LBN (logical block number)
0x1c000a54e  mov     ecx, dword [rax+0x1b0]        ; ecx = sector shift
0x1c000a554  shl     rdx, cl                       ; rdx = LBN << sector_shift (byte offset)
0x1c000a557  mov     qword [rsi+r9*8], rdx         ; array[index].starting_offset = byte offset
0x1c000a55b  mov     r8d, dword [r10+0xc]          ; r8d = dirent data length
0x1c000a55f  mov     qword [rsi+r9*8+0x8], r8      ; array[index].data_length = data length
0x1c000a564  cmp     byte [r10+0x18], 0x0          ; check interleave flag
0x1c000a569  jl      0x1c000a584
0x1c000a56b  mov     rcx, qword [rdi+0x68]
0x1c000a56f  add     r8d, dword [rcx+0x1b8]
0x1c000a576  mov     eax, dword [rcx+0x1bc]
0x1c000a57c  and     r8, rax
0x1c000a57f  mov     qword [rsi+r9*8+0x8], r8
0x1c000a584  mov     qword [rsi+r9*8+0x10], r14    ; array[index].file_offset = arg4
0x1c000a589  mov     ecx, dword [r10+0x1c]         ; ecx = interleave unit size
0x1c000a58d  test    ecx, ecx
0x1c000a58f  je      0x1c000a5bb
0x1c000a591  mov     rax, qword [rdi+0x68]
0x1c000a595  mov     edx, ecx
0x1c000a597  mov     ecx, dword [rax+0x1b0]
0x1c000a59d  shl     rdx, cl
0x1c000a5a0  mov     qword [rsi+r9*8+0x18], rdx
0x1c000a5a5  mov     rax, qword [rdi+0x68]
0x1c000a5a9  mov     r8d, dword [r10+0x20]         ; r8d = interleave gap size
0x1c000a5ad  mov     ecx, dword [rax+0x1b0]
0x1c000a5b3  shl     r8, cl
0x1c000a5b6  add     r8, rdx
0x1c000a5b9  jmp     0x1c000a5c0
0x1c000a5bb  mov     qword [rsi+r9*8+0x18], r8
0x1c000a5c0  mov     qword [rsi+r9*8+0x20], r8     ; array[index].extent_end
0x1c000a5c5  lea     eax, [rbp+0x1]                ; result = index + 1
0x1c000a5c8  mov     rbx, qword [rsp+0x30]
0x1c000a5cd  mov     rbp, qword [rsp+0x38]
0x1c000a5d2  mov     rsi, qword [rsp+0x40]
0x1c000a5d7  mov     dword [rdi+0x10c], eax        ; store dirent count
0x1c000a5dd  mov     rdi, qword [rsp+0x48]
0x1c000a5e2  add     rsp, 0x20
0x1c000a5e6  pop     r14
0x1c000a5e8  retn

breakpoints.why: Entry to the vulnerable function. At entry: rdx (arg2) = FCB pointer, r8d (arg3) = dirent index being added. Check [rdx+0x108] (capacity) — if it is 0x4000000 (2^26), the next growth will overflow. Watch r8d approaching the capacity to know when growth triggers.

The vulnerable 32-bit size computation: lea eax,[rax+rax*4]. At this point eax = capacity (from [rdx+0x108]). If eax == 0x4000000 (the first power-of-two capacity above the ~53.7M overflow threshold), the subsequent shl eax,0x4 at 0x1c000a4a6 overflows (0x140000000 truncates to 0x40000000). Step to 0x1c000a4ae to see the truncated allocation size in edx.

The overflow-causing memmove call. At this point: rcx = destination buffer (undersized pool allocation), rdx = source (old array), r8 = copy length (capacity * 0x28 in 64-bit, NOT overflowed). If r8 exceeds the actual allocation size, the pool overflow occurs here. Set a breakpoint and compare r8 to the allocation size from the ExAllocatePoolWithTag return.

ExAllocatePoolWithTag call. edx = truncated allocation size. For the overflowing capacity 0x4000000, this is 0x40000000 (1 GiB) — far smaller than the true 64-bit size 0x140000000. Note the return value in rax (the pool pointer) — this is the buffer that will be overflowed.

watch: eax at 0x1c000a485 = capacity from FCB+0x108 (the count that drives the overflow; watch for the value 0x4000000)
edx at 0x1c000a4ae = truncated allocation size passed to ExAllocatePoolWithTag (should be capacity*0x50 but wraps in 32-bit)
r8 at 0x1c000a4e6 = memmove copy length = capacity * 0x28 (64-bit, full value — compare against the allocation size to confirm overflow)
rcx at 0x1c000a4e6 = destination buffer pointer (the undersized pool allocation that gets overflowed)
rdx at 0x1c000a4e6 = source old array pointer (valid, contains capacity entries of dirent data controlled by attacker)
FCB+0x108 = allocation array capacity (doubles each growth: 1,2,4,...,2^n)
key_offsets: 0x1c000a49d: lea eax,[rax+rax*4] — THE BUG: 32-bit multiply for allocation size (should be 64-bit lea r10,...)
0x1c000a4a6: shl eax,0x4 — THE BUG: 32-bit shift completes the overflow (capacity*0x50 wraps)
0x1c000a4db: lea r8,[rcx+rcx*4] — 64-bit multiply for memmove size (correct, does not overflow)
0x1c000a4e2: shl r8,0x3 — 64-bit shift: final memmove size = capacity*0x28
0x1c000a4e6: call memmove — THE OVERFLOW SITE: writes capacity*0x28 bytes into truncated allocation
In PATCHED binary at 0x1c000a4a1-0x1c000a4b7: the added overflow check (lea r10,[rax+rax*4]; shl r10,0x4; cmp r10,rdx; cmovbe eax,r10d; ja error_path) that was ENTIRELY ABSENT in unpatched
In PATCHED binary at 0x1c000a5fa: CdRaiseStatusEx(STATUS_UNEXPECTED_IO_ERROR) — the error path that prevents the overflow
debugging_aids.trigger_setup:
1) Create a crafted ISO9660 disk image containing a file with an enormous number of directory entry extents. Use ISO9660 interleaved extents (small File Unit Size + Interleave Gap Size) or many separate directory records for the same file to generate on the order of 67 million dirents (index must reach 2^26).
2) The ISO image must be large enough to hold the directory records (each dirent is ~33-48+ bytes; ~67M dirents ≈ 2-3+ GB of directory data). A large disk image is required.
3) Mount the ISO image: use Windows 10/11 built-in ISO mounting (double-click .iso), or any virtual optical drive software, or burn to physical optical media.
4) Open a file on the mounted volume via CreateFile() or simply browse to it in Explorer — this triggers IRP_MJ_CREATE → CdFsdDispatch → CdCommonCreate → CdOpenFileFromFileContext → CdInitializeFcbFromFileContext → CdAddAllocationFromDirent.
5) Alternatively, reading from the file triggers IRP_MJ_READ → CdCommonRead → CdNonCachedRead → CdPrepareBuffers → CdLookupAllocation → CdAddAllocationFromDirent.
6) As dirents are processed, the capacity counter at FCB+0x108 doubles until it reaches 0x4000000 (2^26). The prior growth at 0x2000000 must first succeed (a ~2.5 GiB paged-pool allocation); at 0x4000000 the 32-bit allocation size computation overflows and the subsequent memmove corrupts the kernel pool.
debugging_aids.expected_observation:
When the overflow triggers, the memmove at 0x1c000a4e6 will write ~2.5 GiB into the 1 GiB pool allocation, overrunning it by ~1.5 GiB. Expected outcomes:
- BSOD with BUGCODE_INVALID_DRIVER (0x000000C4) or BAD_POOL_CALLER (0x000000C2) or PAGE_FAULT_IN_NONPAGED_AREA (0x00000050) as the copy runs off the end of the pool region into unmapped memory.
- In WinDbg with !pool or !analyze -v after the crash, the faulting address will be in the paged pool region, past the allocation returned by ExAllocatePoolWithTag at 0x1c000a4b2.
- The call stack will show CdAddAllocationFromDirent → memmove → crash.
debugging_aids.notes:
The capacity at FCB+0x108 is a 32-bit unsigned integer initialized to 1 and doubled each time the array grows (1→2→4→...→2^n). It is NOT directly read from disk — it grows organically as directory entries are parsed, so it only ever takes power-of-two values. For the overflow at capacity=0x4000000 (2^26), the index must reach 2^26, i.e. on the order of 67 million dirents are needed.

The Vcb (Volume Control Block) is at FCB+0x68. Relevant Vcb fields:
- Vcb+0x1b0 = SectorShift (typically 11 for 2048-byte sectors)
- Vcb+0x1b8 = SectorOffset mask adjustment
- Vcb+0x1bc = SectorSize mask

Each allocation array entry is 0x28 bytes (5 qwords): [0]=starting_byte_offset, [8]=data_length, [0x10]=file_offset, [0x18]=interleave_adjusted_offset, [0x20]=extent_end. The attacker controls these values via crafted dirent fields in the ISO9660 image.

The two other changed functions (CdLookupAllocation, CdInitializeFcbFromFileContext) have purely supporting changes: register reallocation and passing arg1 (IRP context) correctly to CdAddAllocationFromDirent so the new CdRaiseStatusEx error path works. These are NOT separate vulnerabilities.

This vulnerability requires no special privileges — any user who can mount or access an ISO image can trigger it. On Windows 10/11, standard users can mount ISO images by double-clicking them.

summary: The patch fixes a critical integer overflow vulnerability (CWE-190) in CdAddAllocationFromDirent (cdfs.sys, the Windows CD-ROM File System driver). The unpatched code computes the kernel pool allocation size for a file's allocation extent array using 32-bit arithmetic (lea eax,[rax+rax*4]; shl eax,0x4), which silently wraps when the extent count exceeds ~53.7 million. The subsequent memmove uses 64-bit arithmetic for the copy length, writing gigabytes of attacker-controlled directory entry data into the undersized pool buffer — a massive kernel pool buffer overflow. A PoC would craft an ISO9660 image with a file containing tens of millions of directory extents (achievable via interleaved extents in a large disk image), mount it, and open/read the file to trigger the overflow during FCB initialization or allocation lookup. The patch changes the multiplication to 64-bit (lea r10,...) and adds an explicit bounds check that raises STATUS_UNEXPECTED_IO_ERROR if the product exceeds 0xFFFFFFFF.
